title: Linial's Problem on Topological Turán Numbers for 3-Graphs
statement_text: For any 3-graph $H$, does there exist a constant $C_H > 0$ such that any $n$-vertex 3-graph with at least $C_H n^{\alpha}$ edges (for some universal exponent $\alpha$ independent of $H$) contains a homeomorphic copy of $H$?
context_text: This is the 3-graph analogue of Mader's classical theorem for graphs. Linial raised this question in 2006 as part of the high-dimensional combinatorics programme; while answers were known for a few specific 3-graphs, no general result in the spirit of Mader's theorem was previously known.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 1.1, establishing that the universal exponent $\alpha = 3 - 1/5$ suffices: any $n$-vertex 3-graph with at least $C_H n^{3-1/5}$ edges contains a homeomorph of $H$, with $C_H = 2000\,v(H)^6$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
notes: The problem is not stated as a formal labelled environment in the paper; it is described in the introduction as 'an old problem of Linial from 2006' with citations [8,9]. The exact original formulation is paraphrased here.

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: For each 3-graph $H$, there exists $C_H > 0$ such that any 3-graph $G$ on $n$ vertices with at least $C_H n^{5/2}$ edges contains a homeomorph of $H$.
context_text: The paper proves the exponent $3-1/5$ is universal but does not believe it to be tight. The conjectured optimal exponent $5/2 = 3 - 1/2$ is motivated by the Brown–Erdős–Sós result that $5/2$ is the correct exponent for the sphere $S^2$ (i.e., $K^3_4$), and by the analogy that homeomorphs in 3-graphs should appear roughly once spheres (homeomorphs of $S^2$) can be found, just as homeomorphs in graphs appear roughly once cycles (homeomorphs of $S^1$) can be found.

title: Torus Homeomorph Turán Problem (Linial)
statement_text: Determine the minimum number of edges in an $n$-vertex 3-graph that guarantees a homeomorphic copy of a triangulation of the torus.
context_text: This is the specialisation of Conjecture 1.2 to $H$ being a triangulation of the torus. The authors note that even this specific case remains open and has been reiterated by Linial on multiple occasions as a natural starting point, since the Brown–Erdős–Sós arguments are specific to 'double-pyramidal' complexes such as the sphere.
paper_contribution: Theorem 1.1 implies the torus homeomorph Turán number is $O(n^{3-1/5})$; the conjectured correct order is $\Theta(n^{5/2})$.
notes: Stated in prose without a labelled environment; attributed to Linial via citations [8,9] but no specific year is recoverable from the provided text.
